era_context: The early-to-mid 14th century: the Hundred Years' War is beginning (1337); the Black Death is spreading along trade routes; the Yuan Dynasty is fragmenting; the Mamluk Sultanate is at its height; and European feudalism is under structural stress.

events.title: Hundred Years' War: Ongoing campaigns and skirmishes
events.description: In 1341, the Hundred Years' War (1337–1453) was in its early phase. Edward III of England had claimed the French throne in 1337, and military campaigns were ongoing in Aquitaine and Brittany. In 1341, there were skirmishes and naval engagements, but no major pitched battles. The conflict was driven by dynastic claims, territorial disputes, and economic competition. The war would define European politics for over a century.
events.certainty_note: The broader conflict is well-documented; specific 1341 events are less detailed.

events.title: Black Death spreads in Asia; plague in Central Asia and China
events.description: The bubonic plague (Yersinia pestis) was spreading along Silk Road trade routes in 1341. Chinese sources document plague deaths in Central Asia and possibly in China during this period. The exact chronology is debated, but by 1341, the pandemic was well-established in Asia. The disease would reach the Mediterranean by 1347, devastating Europe.

sources.name: Yuan Shi and Chinese local records

events.certainty_note: Plague's presence in Asia by 1341 is supported by Chinese sources, though exact dating varies.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty under Emperor Shun; internal conflict and famine
events.description: In 1341, the Yuan Dynasty under Emperor Shun (Toghon Temür, r. 1333–1368) was experiencing severe internal conflict and famine. Factional struggles among Mongol nobility, Han Chinese officials, and regional warlords were destabilizing the empire. Peasant unrest was growing. The empire would ultimately collapse to the Ming Dynasty (1368). In 1341, the Yuan was still nominally powerful but increasingly fragile.
key_figures: Toghon Temür (Emperor Shun of Yuan, r. 1333–1368)

sources.name: Yuan Shi (History of the Yuan, compiled by Ming historians)
Chinese administrative documents and local records

events.certainty_note: Yuan instability in the 1340s is well-documented; specific 1341 events are less detailed.

events.title: Mali Empire under Mansa Maghan I or Mansa Sulayman; West African trade
events.description: In 1341, Mali was ruled by either Mansa Maghan I or Mansa Sulayman (succession dates are disputed). Mali was a major power in West African gold trade and Islamic scholarship. The empire controlled trans-Saharan trade routes and maintained diplomatic relations with North African and Middle Eastern powers. In 1341, Mali's influence was still strong, though the empire was beginning to face competition from rising regional powers.
key_figures: Mansa Maghan I or Mansa Sulayman (succession dates disputed)

sources.name: Tarikh al-Sudan and Tarikh al-Fattash (West African chronicles)

events.certainty_note: Mali's political history in the 1340s relies on oral tradition and later chronicles. Succession dates are disputed.

events.title: Mamluk Sultanate under al-Nasir Muhammad; Eastern Mediterranean dominance
events.description: In 1341, the Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt under al-Nasir Muhammad (r. 1310–1341) was at the height of its power. Al-Nasir Muhammad had consolidated Mamluk control over Egypt, the Levant, and the Hijaz (including the holy cities of Mecca and Medina). He was known for his military strength, his patronage of architecture and Islamic learning, and his diplomatic relations with European and Asian powers. In 1341, al-Nasir Muhammad died, marking the end of an era of Mamluk stability.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Kitab al-Suluk (History of the Mamluks, written early 15th century)
Contemporary Islamic chronicles and administrative records

events.certainty_note: Al-Nasir Muhammad's death in 1341 is well-documented in Islamic sources.

disconfirming_evidence: Mali's rulers in the 1340s are often conflated or misnamed; the exact succession and dates are disputed. The plague's spread in 1341 is less well-documented than in 1344–1347; some scholars argue the pandemic's major impact in Asia occurred slightly later. Al-Nasir Muhammad's death in 1341 is sometimes dated to 1340 in older sources.
historiographic_note: 1341 is a year of gradual change rather than dramatic events. European sources focus on the Hundred Years' War. Chinese sources document Yuan instability. Islamic sources document the Mamluk Sultanate. West African history relies on oral tradition and later chronicles, with significant gaps. The plague's spread is documented retrospectively. Bias toward European, East Asian, and Islamic history is evident; African and Southeast Asian developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Early Hundred Years' War campaigns in 1341 led to the Battle of Crécy in 1346
Plague spread in Asia in 1341 reached the Mediterranean in 1347
Al-Nasir Muhammad's death in 1341 occurred shortly before Clement VI's election in 1342
